python.code:
def scalar_multiply(matrix: list[list[int|float]], scalar: int|float) -> list[list[int|float]]:
    return [[element * scalar for element in row] for row in matrix]
python.explanation:
Scalar multiplication applies a single value uniformly to every element in the matrix. This is one of the most fundamental linear algebra operations — it scales the magnitude of all entries without changing the matrix's structure or relative proportions between elements.

The implementation uses a nested list comprehension: the outer comprehension iterates over each row, and the inner one multiplies each element in that row by the scalar. This produces a brand-new matrix of the same dimensions with all values scaled.

This approach preserves the original matrix (no in-place mutation), which is generally preferred in functional and mathematical contexts. The time complexity is O(m×n) where m and n are the matrix dimensions, and space complexity is also O(m×n) for the output.
key_learnings: Scalar multiplication is element-wise — every entry is multiplied by the same value, making it O(m×n) with no shortcuts
Nested list comprehensions are idiomatic Python for 2D matrix transformations and are faster than equivalent nested for-loops
Multiplying by 0 produces a zero matrix; multiplying by 1 is an identity operation — good edge cases to mention in interviews
This operation is linear: scalar_multiply(A+B, k) == scalar_multiply(A, k) + scalar_multiply(B, k), a property that underpins much of linear algebra
Always return a new matrix rather than mutating the input — interviewers often probe whether candidates think about side effects

numpy.explanation:
NumPy overloads the `*` operator for arrays to perform element-wise scalar multiplication via broadcasting. When you multiply an ndarray by a Python scalar, NumPy broadcasts the scalar across all dimensions automatically — no explicit looping required.

Under the hood, NumPy executes this operation in optimized C/FORTRAN code using SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) CPU instructions, making it orders of magnitude faster than pure Python for large matrices. The operation is vectorized: the entire array is processed in contiguous memory blocks rather than one element at a time.

The `.tolist()` call converts the result back to a plain Python list to match the function signature. In practice, you would keep the result as an ndarray and avoid this conversion for any further numerical work.
key_learnings: NumPy broadcasting rules allow a scalar (0-D) to multiply against any-dimensional array without explicit loops
NumPy stores data in contiguous C-order memory by default, enabling SIMD vectorization that pure Python lists cannot leverage
The `*` operator on ndarrays is element-wise; use `np.dot` or `@` for matrix multiplication — a common interview confusion point
Avoid unnecessary `.tolist()` conversions in real code; keep data as ndarrays to avoid O(m×n) Python object overhead
For very large matrices, consider `np.multiply(matrix, scalar, out=result)` to write into a pre-allocated buffer and avoid extra memory allocation
